spans.text: Participants were told that the purpose of the vote was to encourage more non-fiction reading among university community members. If participants agreed to cast a vote, they were handed an iPad with an initial screen showing them a list of 25 well-known biographical books to choose from (e.g.,
Anne Frank: The Diary of a Young Girl; Steve Jobs: A Biography

). Above the title of each biography were a thumbnail picture of its cover and a brief description including the name of the book’s protagonist, a description of the protagonist (e.g., diarist during the Holocaust; Apple co-founder), the year the book was published, and the book’s page count (e.g., 283 pages, 656 pages). Of the biographies presented, 5 (20%) featured female protagonists.

spans.text: These results were robust to several alternative specifications, including controlling for participant gender, race, and age (see Appendix Table S1).

To formally test whether offering feedback about the percentage of past experts who were women had a larger effect on the subsequent selection of women experts than offering feedback about other attributes had on the subsequent selection of experts with those attributes, we constructed three systems of simultaneous equations (Zellner, 1962). For each system, we included a pair of equations: one modeling the effect of random assignment to receive gender feedback and one modeling the effect of random assignment to receive feedback on another expert attribute (i.e., past selectees who were under 50 years old, based on the West Coast, or worked at a university, see Table 2, Models 5-8). We then used Wald tests to compare the treatment effects estimated in the paired equations, which confirmed that the effect of assignment to the gender feedback condition on the proportion of women experts selected was significantly larger than the effects of assignment to receive descriptive feedback about the percentage of past experts who were (1) under 50 years old (
< .001) on subsequent selections of experts with those respective attributes. This pattern of findings held across stimuli sets.

phaseA.text: These results were robust to several alternative specifications, including controlling for participant gender, race, and age (see Appendix Table S1).	To formally test whether offering feedback about the percentage of past experts who were women had a larger effect on the subsequent selection of women experts than offering feedback about other attributes had on the subsequent selection of experts with those attributes, we constructed three systems of simultaneous equations (Zellner, 1962). For each system, we included a pair of equations: one modeling the effect of random assignment to receive gender feedback and one modeling the effect of random assignment to receive feedback on another expert attribute (i.e., past selectees who were under 50 years old, based on the West Coast, or worked at a university, see Table 2, Models 5-8). We then used Wald tests to compare the treatment effects estimated in the paired equations, which confirmed that the effect of assignment to the gender feedback condition on the proportion of women experts selected was significantly larger than the effects of assignment to receive descriptive feedback about the percentage of past experts who were (1) under 50 years old (F(1, 3988) = 6.63, p = .010), (2) based on the West Coast (F(1, 3988) = 8.35, p = .004), or (3) worked at a university (F(1, 3988) = 12.84, p < .001) on subsequent selections of experts with those respective attributes. This pattern of findings held across stimuli sets.

spans.text: According to Harrison & List (2004, p. 1014), a framed field experiment is “one that uses participants from the specific population of interest (a nonstandard subject pool) and incorporates field context into the commodity, task, or information participants use, making the setting more realistic than a conventional lab experiment.”

Our framed field experiment extends our prior findings by examining whether they apply in a setting where people were making real decisions with real impact on their community and were unaware that they were in a research study. Specifically, h

spans.text: First, we demonstrate that the effect of descriptive feedback on selection decisions persists in a field setting where participants made consequential choices affecting their community—voting on which biographies would be publicly displayed on a university campus. Second, unlike in Study 1 for which participants were told about hypothetical prior selections, participants in Study 2 made their own initial selections before receiving feedback, confirming that the effect arises even when evaluators reflect on their own past choices.

phaseA.text: First, we demonstrate that the effect of descriptive feedback on selection decisions persists in a field setting where participants made consequential choices affecting their community—voting on which biographies would be publicly displayed on a university campus. Second, unlike in Study 1 for which participants were told about hypothetical prior selections, participants in Study 2 made their own initial selections before receiving feedback, confirming that the effect arises even when evaluators reflect on their own past choices.

phaseB.footnote_text: We only recruited participants who were U.S. based, had a minimum 90% approval rate, and at least 1,000 prior survey completions. In all future Prolific studies reported in this manuscript, we imposed these same requirements.

phaseB.footnote_text: We also stimulus sampled what descriptive feedback participants received about their past sources: They were either told that 10% (or 20%) of past experts were women, that 30% (or 50%) were under 50 years old, that 10% (or 70%) were based on the West Coast, and that 50% (or 10%) worked at a university.

Results

phaseC.anchor_search: We only recruited participants who were U.S. based
phaseC.comment_body: Katy's comment: Jose, cutting this level of detail means your study could not be replicated as performed, so this isn't really Kosher. You can put it in a footnote and say it just once though assuming you use the same threshold everywhere. If you would just use the same threshold, you could change the footnote to say something like "unless otherwise stated, all other studies in this manuscript also…" and then put other footnotes to indicate other rules.

phaseC.anchor_search: In Study 2, we conducted a framed field experiment
phaseC.comment_body: Katy's comment: Without my additions, it's totally unclear what DV you're even measuring in the comparisons so really doesn't work. Honestly, my advice would be to leave the detailed write up in Study 1 so it appears once and then to switch to this condensed form in Study 2. I really don't like the lack of clarity here and suspect George was asking you to streamline LATER studies and avoid repetition. I can't imagine he really wants you to just wave your hands about how you found key results.

phaseC.anchor_search: The IRB approved a waiver of informed consent

Katy's comment: Report the same demographics each time. I don't care what they are, but be consistent.

phaseC.comment_body: Katy's comment: I would revert to the prior writeup. I don't think this rewrite is sufficiently clear and it's not much shorter. Fine to move all the specific F-tests to a table in the appendix and just say "all p's > XX" instead of giving each, but honestly, I think that may be creating more complexity than it's sparing the reader.

phaseC.anchor_search: Wald tests employed, all ps ≤ .010; see Table 2).

Katy's comment (needs review): I left an "X" placeholder for the appendix table/section that contains the full baseline-balance statistics. Jose: please fill in the correct table/section reference.

phaseC.anchor_search: Study 2 extends our findings in two important ways.
phaseC.comment_body: Katy's comment (needs review): Katy's PDF green text suggests rewriting this Discussion paragraph with explicit "First, we find that the effect persists..." and "Second, here participants received feedback about their own prior selections..." structure. The exact final wording was uncertain in the cross-check. Jose: review and refine.
